title: Label, relate, solve: geometry and trigonometry

objectives.statement: Calculate and scale lengths, perimeters, areas, and volumes while tracking the power of the linear scale factor.
Use the Pythagorean relation and right-triangle trigonometric ratios to solve labeled triangles and check bounds.
Convert between degrees and radians and interpret sine and cosine as unit-circle coordinates.
Use inverse trigonometric functions to recover principal angles and interpret them within a stated geometric quadrant or domain.

scenes.title: Perpendicular lengths do not simply add
scenes.contentHtml:
Perpendicular lengths do not simply add

A fictional locator reports an object 12 units east and 5 units north of the origin. Its software reports a straight-line distance of 17 units.

The components are perpendicular, so they form the legs of a right triangle. The distance dd is

d=122+52=169=13,d=\sqrt{12^2+5^2}=\sqrt{169}=13,

not 12+512+5. The hypotenuse must be longer than either leg, and recomputing 132=122+5213^2=12^2+5^2 independently checks the result.

This lesson uses a four-part geometric audit: label the quantities and angle units, choose a relation that matches the shape, solve, then check lengths, signs, scale, and units.

Starting check

  • If every length doubles, by what factor does area change?
  • Is 180∘180^\circ equal to π\pi radians or 2π2\pi radians?
  • In a right triangle, can a leg be longer than the hypotenuse?

If the spatial description is difficult, list the points and perpendicular relationships in text before writing an equation.

Source Note
claims claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-angle-conversionsources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-si-angle

NASA supports the right-triangle relation and NIST supports the degree-radian conversion. The locator and audit are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every essential shape is represented with named points, perpendicular relationships, coordinates, and equations. No task requires seeing a diagram, judging an angle visually, using color, or dragging labels.

scenes.title: Connect shape, scale, and angle
scenes.contentHtml:
Connect shape, scale, and angle

Geometry relates measurements to structure. If a shape is enlarged by linear factor kk, corresponding lengths scale by kk, areas by k2k^2, and volumes by k3k^3. The powers come from how many independent length dimensions are multiplied: a rectangle changes from lwlw to (kl)(kw)=k2lw(kl)(kw)=k^2lw, while a rectangular solid changes from lwhlwh to (kl)(kw)(kh)=k3lwh(kl)(kw)(kh)=k^3lwh. The same dimensional count applies to similar figures and solids.

For a right triangle with legs a,ba,b and hypotenuse cc,

a2+b2=c2.a^2+b^2=c^2.

Relative to an acute angle θ\theta:

sin⁡θ=oppositehypotenuse,cos⁡θ=adjacenthypotenuse,tan⁡θ=oppositeadjacent.\sin\theta=\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}},\quad \cos\theta=\frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}},\quad \tan\theta=\frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}.

The labels “opposite” and “adjacent” depend on the chosen angle; the hypotenuse is always opposite the right angle. The ratios also show that tan⁡θ=sin⁡θ/cos⁡θ\tan\theta=\sin\theta/\cos\theta wherever cos⁡θ≠0\cos\theta\ne0.

Definition
id definition-radian

One radian is the angle subtending an arc whose length equals the circle radius. A full turn is 2π2\pi radians, so 180∘=π180^\circ=\pi radians and 1∘=π/1801^\circ=\pi/180 radians.

Unit-circle meaning

On a unit circle centered at the origin, the point reached by angle θ\theta measured counterclockwise from the positive horizontal axis has coordinates

(cos⁡θ,sin⁡θ).(\cos\theta,\sin\theta).

This extends sine and cosine beyond acute triangles and supplies signs by quadrant. The unit-circle equation is x2+y2=1x^2+y^2=1. Substituting x=cos⁡θx=\cos\theta and y=sin⁡θy=\sin\theta gives cos2⁡θ+sin2⁡θ=1\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1.

Recovering an angle

If a right triangle has opposite/adjacent ratio rr, then θ=arctan⁡(r)\theta=\arctan(r) returns a principal angle. For real inputs, the usual principal ranges are [−90∘,90∘][-90^\circ,90^\circ] for arcsine, [0∘,180∘][0^\circ,180^\circ] for arccosine, and (−90∘,90∘)(-90^\circ,90^\circ) for arctangent. Calculator angle mode must match the requested unit.

For a standard-position direction from coordinates (x,y)(x,y), the signs identify the quadrant. When x≠0x\ne0, first find the acute reference angle α=arctan⁡(|y/x|)\alpha=\arctan(|y/x|), then use α\alpha in quadrant I, 180∘−α180^\circ-\alpha in quadrant II, 180∘+α180^\circ+\alpha in quadrant III, or 360∘−α360^\circ-\alpha in quadrant IV. If x=0x=0, the direction is 90∘90^\circ for y>0y>0 or 270∘270^\circ for y<0y<0. For example, (−3,4)(-3,4) gives α≈53.13∘\alpha\approx53.13^\circ but a quadrant-II direction of about 126.87∘126.87^\circ. A raw one-argument arctangent cannot choose that quadrant from the ratio alone.

Source Note
claims claim-geometric-scaling, claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-right-triangle-ratios, claim-trig-functions, claim-angle-conversion, claim-unit-circle, claim-inverse-trig, claim-quadrant-directionsources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-dlmf-trig, source-nist-si-angle

NASA supports geometric dimensions, right-triangle relations, ratios, and coordinate conversion; NIST supports trigonometric principal values and exact degree-radian conversion. The explanations and examples are original synthesis.

scenes.title: Recover a distance and direction
scenes.contentHtml:
Recover a distance and direction

A fictional beacon lies at coordinate (9,12)(9,12) relative to an origin. Find its straight-line distance and its counterclockwise angle from the positive horizontal axis.

Worked Example
id worked-example-beacon

1. Label. Horizontal leg 9, vertical leg 12, both positive; the point lies in the first quadrant.

2. Distance.

d=92+122=225=15.d=\sqrt{9^2+12^2}=\sqrt{225}=15.

3. Direction.

tan⁡θ=129=43,\tan\theta=\frac{12}{9}=\frac43,

so θ=arctan⁡(4/3)≈53.13∘≈0.9273 rad\theta=\arctan(4/3)\approx53.13^\circ\approx0.9273\text{ rad}.

4. Check ratios. cos⁡θ=9/15=0.6\cos\theta=9/15=0.6 and sin⁡θ=12/15=0.8\sin\theta=12/15=0.8; 0.62+0.82=10.6^2+0.8^2=1.

5. Check bounds and quadrant. The distance 15 exceeds both component lengths, and 152=92+12215^2=9^2+12^2; both coordinates are positive, so the acute first-quadrant angle is consistent.

Check
id check-angle-mode

If a calculator returns approximately 0.9273, why is reporting 0.9273∘0.9273^\circ wrong?

The value is in radians. It must be labeled as radians or converted to about 53.13∘53.13^\circ.

Check
id check-model-choice

Why was tangent the direct ratio for the direction, and how do the coordinate signs constrain the answer?

The two known legs are opposite and adjacent to the requested angle, so tangent uses them without first finding the hypotenuse. Both coordinates are positive, so the standard-position direction must be in quadrant I.

Source Note
claims claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-right-triangle-ratios, claim-angle-conversion, claim-unit-circlesources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-si-angle

The beacon, values, solution, and checks are original applications of the sourced geometric and angle-unit relations.

scenes.title: Inverse sine is not reciprocal sine
scenes.contentHtml:
Inverse sine is not reciprocal sine

Commit first

Without calculating, decide whether sin−1⁡(0.5)\sin^{-1}(0.5) should return an angle or a ratio. Write your prediction before continuing.

Misconception
id misconception-inverse-reciprocal

Tempting shortcut

“The exponent −1-1 always means reciprocal, so sin−1⁡(x)=1/sin⁡(x)\sin^{-1}(x)=1/\sin(x).”

Why the shortcut fails

The two operations have different input and output types. The inverse function arcsin⁡(x)\arcsin(x) takes a ratio and returns a principal angle. The reciprocal 1/sin⁡(x)1/\sin(x) takes an angle and returns a ratio.

With a ratio input of 0.50.5,

arcsin⁡(0.5)=30∘=π/6,\arcsin(0.5)=30^\circ=\pi/6,

whereas using an angle input of 0.50.5 radians gives

1sin⁡(0.5 rad)≈2.0858.\frac{1}{\sin(0.5\ \mathrm{rad})}\approx2.0858.

The unequal outputs—and their different units—disprove the shortcut.

Repair

Read sin−1⁡(x)\sin^{-1}(x) as arcsin⁡(x)\arcsin(x) when the task asks for an angle. Write 1/sin⁡(x)1/\sin(x) or csc⁡(x)\csc(x) when the task asks for a reciprocal. Then label the angle unit and use the stated domain or quadrant to interpret the principal result.

Recheck

A calculator shows cos−1⁡(0.25)\cos^{-1}(0.25). Is it finding a reciprocal or an angle, and what input would the reciprocal require?

Check after committing: It is finding the principal angle whose cosine is 0.250.25. The reciprocal calculation would require an angle input and would be written 1/cos⁡(θ)1/\cos(\theta) or sec⁡(θ)\sec(\theta).

Source Note
claims claim-inverse-trigsources source-nist-dlmf-trig

NIST supports inverse-trigonometric definitions and principal values. The repair sequence and numerical contrast are original teaching expression.

scenes.title: Choose the relation from the labels
scenes.contentHtml:
Choose the relation from the labels

For each item, cover or skip past its feedback, write a complete answer, and only then compare your reasoning.

1. Scale a shape

A rectangle's linear dimensions are multiplied by 1.5. What happens to its area?

Feedback after you commit: Area is multiplied by 1.52=2.251.5^2=2.25.

2. Complete a right triangle

The hypotenuse is 10 and one leg is 6. Find the other leg.

Feedback after you commit: 102−62=8\sqrt{10^2-6^2}=8. The leg is below the hypotenuse.

3. Convert angle units

Convert 135∘135^\circ to radians.

Feedback after you commit: 135(π/180)=3π/4135(\pi/180)=3\pi/4 radians.

4. Choose a ratio

Relative to angle θ\theta, the adjacent leg is 7 and hypotenuse is 25. Find θ\theta in degrees.

Feedback after you commit: cos⁡θ=7/25\cos\theta=7/25, so θ=arccos⁡(7/25)≈73.74∘\theta=\arccos(7/25)\approx73.74^\circ.

Recovery route

  • Wrong side labels: name the reference angle before opposite and adjacent.
  • Lengths added instead of squared: state whether they are collinear or perpendicular.
  • Area or volume scaled linearly: apply the scale factor once per dimension.
  • Inverse function confused with reciprocal: write arc notation explicitly.
  • Plausible digits but wrong angle: label degrees or radians before interpreting.
Source Note
claims claim-geometric-scaling, claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-right-triangle-ratios, claim-angle-conversion, claim-inverse-trigsources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-dlmf-trig, source-nist-si-angle

All practice, feedback, and recovery guidance are original.

scenes.title: Audit a rotated panel
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit a rotated panel

A fictional rigid rectangular panel is 12 units long and 5 units wide. Its 12-unit edge is tilted 30∘30^\circ above the positive horizontal direction, and its perpendicular 5-unit edge points toward the upper left. The panel rests with its lowest corner at height zero. Decide whether its highest corner fits below an opening 10 units high. Then predict the height and area factors for a similar panel with every length multiplied by 0.8.

Feedback after a complete attempt

This is not one right-triangle component. Both perpendicular edges contribute to the height. The 12-unit edge contributes

12sin⁡30∘=6,12\sin30^\circ=6,

and the 5-unit edge is at 120∘120^\circ, so its upward contribution is

5sin⁡120∘=5cos⁡30∘≈4.33.5\sin120^\circ=5\cos30^\circ\approx4.33.

The total height is about 10.3310.33 units, so the panel exceeds the opening by about 0.330.33 unit. A coordinate check for the long edge gives horizontal and vertical components (12cos⁡30∘,12sin⁡30∘)≈(10.39,6)(12\cos30^\circ,12\sin30^\circ)\approx(10.39,6), whose squared lengths sum to 12212^2.

Scaling every length by 0.8 makes the height factor 0.8, so the new height is about 8.268.26 units. Area has two length dimensions, so its factor is 0.82=0.640.8^2=0.64; the original area is 60 square units and the scaled area is 38.4 square units. The angle is 30∘=π/630^\circ=\pi/6 radians, and arctan⁡(6/10.39)\arctan(6/10.39) reconstructs the first-quadrant direction as about 30∘30^\circ.

The context is fictional and the calculation is not engineering certification.

Source Note
claims claim-geometric-scaling, claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-right-triangle-ratios, claim-angle-conversion, claim-inverse-trigsources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-dlmf-trig, source-nist-si-angle

The rotated panel, numbers, clearance decision, reconstruction, and boundary statement are original.

scenes.title: Rebuild the geometric audit
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the geometric audit

  1. Name points, perpendicular relations, side labels, scale, and angle unit.
  2. Choose a relation: scaling power, Pythagorean equation, or trigonometric ratio.
  3. Solve symbolically before rounding.
  4. Use inverse trigonometry only after forming a valid ratio.
  5. Check triangle bounds, quadrant signs, unit-circle identity, and units.

Retrieval

  • Why does area scale with the square of a linear factor?
  • How do degrees convert to radians?
  • What are cosine and sine on the unit circle?
  • How does arcsin differ from reciprocal sine?
  • If x<0x<0 and y>0y>0, why is a raw value of arctan⁡(y/x)\arctan(y/x) not yet the standard-position direction?

Complete assessment.json. If side choice fails, return to labeled ratios. If angle mode fails, redo the beacon conversion. If a visual sketch feels essential, replace it with coordinates, named sides, and perpendicular relationships before retrying.

Source Note
claims claim-geometric-scaling, claim-right-triangle-geometry, claim-right-triangle-ratios, claim-angle-conversion, claim-unit-circle, claim-inverse-trig, claim-quadrant-directionsources source-nasa-geometry-trig, source-nist-dlmf-trig, source-nist-si-angle

The synthesis uses sourced geometric, trigonometric, scaling, and angle-unit relations through an original audit method.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. OpenAI Codex, model gpt-5.6-sol, authored, researched, edited, and packaged the frozen candidate in run d6ce8df3-939e-4724-8a5d-d7f48941a174. items.prompt: Every linear dimension of a solid is multiplied by 0.8. Enter the resulting volume factor.

answer.reasoning: Volume contains three independent length dimensions, so the linear scale factor is applied three times.

items.prompt: A right triangle has legs 7 and 24. Find the hypotenuse and give a bound check.

answer.correct: c = √(7² + 24²) = √625 = 25. It is longer than the larger leg 24, and recomputing 25² = 7² + 24² checks the result.
answer.reasoning: Perpendicular legs combine through their squares; the hypotenuse ordering and a direct substitution independently test the result.
commonErrors: Giving 25 without a bound or substitution check

items.prompt: Convert 225° to exact radians and state its quadrant in standard position.

answer.correct: 225° × π/180° = 5π/4 radians. It lies in quadrant III.
answer.reasoning: The angle is 45° beyond 180°, placing both unit-circle coordinates negative.

answer.summary: At 150°, cosine is negative and sine is positive.
answer.reasoning: The reference angle is 30° in quadrant II, so the coordinate magnitudes are √3/2 and 1/2 with signs (-,+).

items.prompt: A point has coordinates (-8, 15) relative to the origin. Find its standard-position direction in degrees to two decimals. Show the reference-angle calculation, use the coordinate signs to interpret the principal result, and give a distance check.

answer.correct: The acute reference angle is α = arctan(15/8) ≈ 61.93°. Because x is negative and y is positive, the point is in quadrant II, so the standard-position direction is 180° - 61.93° = 118.07°. The distance is √(8² + 15²) = 17, which exceeds both component magnitudes.
answer.reasoning: A one-argument arctangent gives a principal value; the coordinate signs select quadrant II, and the 8-15-17 relation independently checks the components.
acceptableResponses: 118.07° with a valid quadrant-II correction and coordinate check

items.prompt: A fictional rigid rectangular panel is 18 units long and 8 units wide. Its 18-unit edge is tilted 32° above the positive horizontal direction, and its perpendicular 8-unit edge points toward the upper left. The lowest corner is at height zero. Determine whether the highest corner fits below a 16-unit opening. Compute the original area and the height and area of a similar panel with linear factor 0.75. Convert 32° to exact radians, reconstruct the angle from the long edge's components, and include geometric and unit checks.

answer.correct: The long edge contributes 18sin32° ≈ 9.539 units vertically. The perpendicular edge contributes 8sin122° = 8cos32° ≈ 6.784 units, so the total height is about 16.323 units and exceeds the opening by about 0.323 unit. The original area is 18×8 = 144 square units. With linear factor 0.75, height is about 12.242 units and area is 144(0.75²) = 81 square units. Also, 32° = 8π/45 radians. The long-edge components are approximately (18cos32°, 18sin32°) = (15.265, 9.539), and arctan(9.539/15.265) ≈ 32°. Their squared lengths sum to 18².
answer.summary: The original height is about 16.323 units, so it does not fit; the scaled height is about 12.242 units and the scaled area is 81 square units.
answer.reasoning: The rotated rectangle requires adding vertical projections of two perpendicular edges, then applying linear and area scale factors separately; component signs, angle reconstruction, and the Pythagorean relation check the model.
acceptableResponses: Values within 0.1 unit or square unit of the key with the two vertical contributions, no-fit decision, 0.75 and 0.75² scaling, 8π/45 radians, reconstruction, and checks
commonErrors: Using only the long edge's vertical component
Subtracting the perpendicular edge's upward contribution
Using 0.75 rather than 0.75² for area scaling
Omitting square units or the clearance decision

criteria.description: Cubes the linear factor and obtains 0.512.
Checks against the larger leg and by direct substitution.
Selects cosine-negative, sine-positive coordinates with correct magnitudes.
Forms the acute reference-angle ratio 15/8 or an equivalent two-component calculation.
Uses quadrant II to obtain about 118.07°.
Checks coordinate signs and the 8-15-17 distance relation.
Models and adds both vertical edge contributions.
Calculates original area and makes the clearance decision with units.
Uses height factor 0.75 and area factor 0.75² for the similar panel.
Checks components with the Pythagorean relation and preserves linear versus square units.

claims.statement: Under a uniform linear scale factor k, corresponding lengths scale by k, areas by k squared, and volumes by k cubed.
claims.scope: Similar plane figures and solids under uniform scaling.
claims.uncertainty: Nonuniform scaling requires treating each changed dimension separately.

claims.statement: For a right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c, a squared plus b squared equals c squared, and the hypotenuse is longer than either leg.
claims.uncertainty: Curved-surface and non-Euclidean geometries are outside scope.

claims.statement: Relative to an acute angle in a right triangle, sine is opposite over hypotenuse, cosine is adjacent over hypotenuse, and tangent is opposite over adjacent.
claims.uncertainty: For general directed angles, signs and coordinates must be interpreted in standard position.

claims.statement: Sine, cosine, and tangent are related trigonometric functions, with tangent equal to sine divided by cosine where cosine is nonzero.
claims.scope: Real-angle trigonometry used in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Complex arguments and analytic continuation are outside scope.

claims.statement: A plane angle of one degree equals pi divided by 180 radians, so a half turn is 180 degrees or pi radians.
claims.uncertainty: Rounding numerical angle values can slightly perturb reconstructed lengths.

claims.statement: For a standard-position real angle, cosine and sine give the horizontal and vertical coordinates on the unit circle; substituting those coordinates into x squared plus y squared equals one gives the Pythagorean identity.
claims.scope: Standard unit-circle interpretation over real angles.
claims.uncertainty: A field using another zero direction or rotation convention must translate to standard position.

claims.statement: Inverse trigonometric functions return principal angles from trigonometric values; inverse-function notation such as sine to the power minus one does not denote the reciprocal sine function.
claims.scope: Real inverse sine, cosine, and tangent calculations in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Software notation can use a superscript minus one ambiguously for inverse versus reciprocal functions.

claims.statement: A principal inverse-trigonometric result does not by itself determine every standard-position direction; coordinate signs or an equivalent quadrant-aware procedure are needed beyond the principal range.
claims.scope: Directions reconstructed from two real Cartesian components.
claims.uncertainty: Axis cases must be handled directly when the horizontal component is zero.

terms.definition: The side opposite the right angle in a right triangle; it is the triangle's longest side.
A plane-angle unit defined by arc length divided by radius; a full turn is 2 pi radians.
For a right-triangle acute angle, opposite divided by hypotenuse; on the unit circle, the vertical coordinate.
For a right-triangle acute angle, adjacent divided by hypotenuse; on the unit circle, the horizontal coordinate.
For a right-triangle acute angle, opposite divided by adjacent; equivalently sine divided by cosine where defined.
A function such as arcsin, arccos, or arctan that returns a principal angle from a trigonometric ratio.
The circle centered at the origin with radius 1; a standard-position angle has endpoint coordinates (cosine, sine) on it.
The acute nonnegative angle between a terminal side and the horizontal axis, used with quadrant signs to interpret a standard-position direction.
The multiplier applied to corresponding lengths; areas and volumes scale by its square and cube respectively.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The geometric-audit structure, locator opening, beacon example, inverse/reciprocal repair, rotated-panel transfer, all coordinates, values, feedback, recovery, and assessments are original. Every applied setting is fictional. NIST and NASA sources provide only standard mathematical facts and relations. No source expression, diagram, table, or example is copied or adapted.

NIST rights and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. NASA public access and media/AI-use guidance were checked on 2026-07-23. No source-specific agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical or engineering advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
